perturbation.original_text: This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.changed_text: This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name). This exception applies even if the third party breached their confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'This exception applies even if the third party breached their confidentiality agreement' weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows disclosure even if the third party breaches confidentiality, which undermines the reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy required to establish a trade secret under UTSA § 1(2).
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'This obligation of confidentiality'

perturbation.justification: The added clause 'This exception applies even if the third party breached their confidentiality agreement' directly undermines the obligation to maintain secrecy, which is crucial for trade secret protection. According to the Law Explanation, this weakens 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy required to establish a trade secret under UTSA § 1(2)'. While there is no specific law snippet to reference, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both explicitly point to this weakening, creating a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Accepted for and on behalf of (a) (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) Signed Dated Signed Dated Signed Dated
perturbation.changed_text: Accepted for and on behalf of (a) (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) Signed Dated. The date of acceptance will be 14 days from today, even if signed earlier. Signed Dated Signed Dated
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the acceptance date to a fixed 14 days undermines the actual date of consent and can cause legal issues with contract enforceability under contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Offer and Acceptance
perturbation.law_explanation: Altering the date of acceptance regardless of the actual signing date contradicts the fundamental principle of offer and acceptance required for contract formation under contract law principles as described in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 17, which mandates a 'meeting of the minds' at a specific time.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a fixed acceptance date (14 days from today), which contradicts the requirement of mutual assent for contract formation. The original text allows for a flexible acceptance date upon signing, aligning with the principle of offer and acceptance. The modified text imposes a predetermined date, regardless of when the parties actually sign, undermining the genuine 'meeting of the minds' at a specific time as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that a contract requires 'mutual assent, expressed by a valid offer and acceptance'. The change undermines this by imposing an acceptance date rather than reflecting actual acceptance.

perturbation.original_text: I/We (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) of (Address) hereby agree to keep confidential any information which has already or may be disclosed to us by: - (b) (Inventor’s name) of (c) (Address)
perturbation.changed_text: I/We (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) of (Address) hereby agree to keep confidential any information which has already or may be disclosed to us by: - (b) (Inventor’s name) of (c) (Address). This obligation to confidentiality expires 90 days after the date this document is signed.
perturbation.explanation: Imposing a short confidentiality period (90 days) may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, conflicting with reasonable protection periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the confidentiality period to just 90 days, the modified agreement fails to provide the 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy required for trade secret protection under UTSA § 2, as it implies the receiving party only needs to keep the information secret for a short time.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day confidentiality period, which directly contradicts the requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy for trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states the modified agreement fails to provide 'reasonable efforts' under UTSA § 2 by limiting confidentiality to 90 days, implying the receiving party only needs to keep the information secret for a short time. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Page Not Found' error from the Uniform Law Commission, this does not negate the strong evidence of contradiction based on the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation that the short confidentiality period fails to provide reasonable protection, making the contract unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: Concerning the (d) (Insert brief description of invention) and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the written approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.changed_text: Concerning the (d) (Insert brief description of invention) and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the written approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name). The inventor's approval is waived if the discloser can prove their intent to disclose was unintentional, and they immediately informed the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing an 'unintentional disclosure' clause weakens confidentiality and contradicts standard trade secret protection expectations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Allowing disclosure based on 'unintentional intent' undermines reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, making it harder to prove misappropriation under UTSA § 1(4), because it introduces ambiguity regarding whether proper precautions were taken.
perturbation.location: Paragraph containing restriction on use or disclosure

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an 'unintentional disclosure' clause, which weakens the confidentiality obligations outlined in the original text. The Law Explanation states that this change undermines 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy'. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the perturbation introduces ambiguity around trade secret protection, creating a legal contradiction under the definition provided for "Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction". The added clause potentially conflicts with standard trade secret protection expectations, making it more difficult to prove misappropriation because it could be argued that the disclosure was unintentional, thereby contradicting the intent to maintain secrecy.
